Modules and packages¶

Python enables us to make our beautiful code available to other people to use by providing mechanisms for implementing modules and packages.

Modules¶

A module is a file (with extension .py) which may contain class definitions, functions and executable code.

Let’s save the following code into a file called greetings.py:

def sayHi():
    print "hello world"

sayHi()

From the python interpreter:

import greetings
  --> hello world

The code in the module greeting is executed the first time that module is imported. Modules are very useful for storing class definitions and sometimes to run some initialization code at import time. Modules can themselves import other modules. There are different methods to import modules, whichever method you use is a matter of preference. Please see idioms and anti-idioms. Certain methods can be better than others. Try to avoid from module import * as this will fill you namespace with function and class names and you don’t know how many:

# good
import sys
print sys.argv

# not as good
from sys import *
print argv

Packages¶

If you create a directory, copy some modules into that directory and create an (even empty) __init__.py file, the contents of that directory is called a package:

mkdir polite
cp greetings.py polite
touch polite/__init__.py

# in your python program
import polite.greetings
polite.greetings.sayHi()

import polite.greetings as p
p.sayHi()

from polite.greetings import sayHi
sayHi()

SemperPy is build by a collection of packages and a lot of attention is given to trying to use the right hierarchy and naming, but once again this is a matter of preference. To read netcdf (or hdf) files which contain gridded data, the class File is in sempery.netcdf.file:

from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
f = File()

# many classes could be called File and there could be a name conflict. It
# could be a better idea to use:
import semperpy.netcdf.file as griddedData
f = griddedData.File()

Now, the __init__.py file is useful to determine what is loaded when importing * from a package. If we put in __init__.py:

__all__ = ['file']

This enables:

from semperpy.netcdf import * # the module file is imported
f = file.File()

If we put in __init__.py:

from file import File

We have:

from semperpy.netcdf import *
f = File()

For more information you can consult the page on modules in the Python documentation.

Some typical imports from semperpy:

from sempery.core.Date import Date, Dates
from sempery.core.tools import is_dict, is_list, to_list
from gmaopy.ods.ods import ODS
import gmaopy.netcdf
from sempery.core.configfile import ConfigFile
